content_en:
The chapter on commercial transactions (buyuu) in al-Umm reflects al-Shafi'i's careful navigation between facilitating legitimate trade and protecting the community from exploitative and uncertain dealings. Islamic commercial law is built on the affirmation of exchange as a human necessity and the concurrent prohibition of specific forms of transaction that generate injustice.

For a sale to be valid in the Shafi'i school, several conditions must be met: the contracting parties must be legally competent (sane, adult, not coerced), the subject matter must exist, be owned by the seller, be deliverable, and be precisely known to both parties. The price must also be known. Ignorance of either the subject matter or the price introduces an element of gharar (uncertainty or deception), which the Prophet explicitly prohibited in the famous hadith: 'The Messenger of Allah forbade the sale of gharar.' Al-Shafi'i treats this prohibition as comprehensive, covering any transaction where the outcome is materially uncertain in a way that could lead to dispute.

Riba, the prohibition on usury and interest, is addressed through al-Shafi'i's analysis of the six ribawi commodities mentioned in the hadith: gold, silver, wheat, barley, dates, and salt. These items may only be exchanged among themselves hand-to-hand and in equal quantities if they are of the same type. Exchanging wheat for wheat in unequal amounts, or on credit, constitutes riba al-fadl (riba of surplus). Any exchange of gold for silver or wheat for barley on credit constitutes riba al-nasi'ah (riba of deferral), even if in equal quantities, because equivalence cannot be ensured across different types.

Al-Shafi'i extends the riba prohibition through analogy to all items sharing the effective causes (illah) he identifies in the six commodities: being measured by weight (for gold, silver) or measured by volume (for the four foodstuffs). He does not, however, extend riba to every edible item, in contrast to the broader Maliki position.

On the options to cancel a sale (khiyar), al-Shafi'i recognizes several types. The option during the session (khiyar al-majlis) allows either party to cancel the sale as long as they remain physically in the same place — a position derived from the hadith: 'The two parties to a sale have the option as long as they have not separated.' This option is accepted by the Shafi'i and Hanbali schools but rejected by the Hanafi and Maliki schools, who interpret 'separation' as meaning the verbal conclusion of the contract rather than physical parting. The option of stipulation (khiyar al-shart) allows either party to insert a period of reflection into the contract — up to three days in the Shafi'i school. The option for defect (khiyar al-ayb) allows the buyer to return goods found to have a pre-existing defect not disclosed at the time of sale.

Al-Shafi'i prohibits several specific types of transaction rooted in pre-Islamic practice: the sale of a fruit crop before its soundness is evident (with some exceptions for necessity), the sale of fish in water, the sale of unborn animals, and the transaction known as muzabanah — exchanging fresh dates still on the tree for dried dates by estimated measure. He permits, however, the exception of 'araya, the sale of fresh dates on the tree for dried dates by estimate, within a specified limit of five wasqs, as this was explicitly permitted by the Prophet to ease genuine need among the poor.
